data.notes: Three-level nested data structure (students within schools within countries). Plausible values used for mathematics ability and self-concept. Constructs measured via Likert-scale surveys; several scales inverted for consistent directionality. Reliability for scales reported with Cronbach’s alpha ranging approximately 0.78–0.89. Data standardized; sample weights applied. Exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses used to validate constructs. Effect sizes computed using Tymms’ method; practical significance threshold emphasized over mere statistical significance due to large N.

method.description: The study used multilevel modeling of PISA 2003 data to examine the Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) for mathematics self-concept and to test moderation by socioeconomic status and academic self-regulation constructs. Mathematics self-concept was measured with a 5-item Likert scale (α = .88). Individual and school-average mathematics ability (plausible values) were included, with cross-level interactions testing 16 potential moderators. Analyses used a three-level structure (students, schools, countries), standardized variables, sample weights, and effect size estimation emphasizing practical over trivial effects.
steps: Acquire PISA 2003 data across 41 countries and apply study exclusions to yield N = 265,180.

Compute/assemble measures: mathematics self-concept (5-item Likert, α = .88), SES indicators (parent occupation/education, home resources, cultural possessions), academic self-regulation constructs (intrinsic/extrinsic motivation, self-efficacy, elaboration, memorization, control strategies), anxiety, and social factors (student–teacher relations, sense of belonging, cooperative/competitive orientations). Invert items where needed for consistent interpretation.
Conduct exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses to validate construct scales; standardize variables where appropriate.

Specify a three-level multilevel model (students nested in schools nested in countries) predicting mathematics self-concept from individual and school-average ability (including a quadratic term for individual ability).
Estimate cross-level interactions between school-average ability (BFLPE) and each of the 16 proposed moderators.

Apply sample weights and use plausible values for mathematics ability; compute effect sizes using Tymms’ method and adopt a stringent significance threshold (p < .001) while prioritizing practical significance.
Interpret main effects and interaction effects, emphasizing robustness and generalizability of the BFLPE and noting moderators with practically meaningful effects.
method.models: Three-level hierarchical linear models (multilevel regression) with random intercepts for schools and countries; fixed effects for individual ability (linear and quadratic), school-average ability (BFLPE), and cross-level interaction terms for proposed moderators. Effect sizes computed using Tymms’ method (Cohen’s d analogue).
method.outcome_variable: Mathematics self-concept (5 items on a Likert scale; Cronbach’s alpha = .88).
method.independent_variables: Individual mathematics ability (plausible values; linear and quadratic), school-average mathematics ability (BFLPE), socioeconomic status indicators (parental education, parent occupation, home educational resources, cultural possessions), motivation (intrinsic, extrinsic), mathematics self-efficacy, learning strategies (elaboration, memorization, control strategies), mathematics anxiety, social relationships (student–teacher relations, sense of belonging), cooperative and competitive orientations, and grade level.
method.control_variables: Random intercepts for schools and countries; sample weights; standardized variables; individual ability (linear and quadratic). Grade level considered; additional controls not explicitly detailed.

results.summary: The study confirmed a robust negative Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE): students of equal ability reported lower mathematics self-concepts when attending higher-ability schools. While numerous moderators were statistically detectable given the large sample, most had small practical effects. Learning strategies and affect showed the most notable moderation, with heavier reliance on memorization and higher mathematics anxiety amplifying the negative BFLPE. Intrinsic/extrinsic motivation and self-efficacy positively predicted self-concept, but their interactions with school-average ability were small or practically unimportant. SES indicators exhibited minimal moderation of the BFLPE. Positive student–teacher relations and sense of belonging were associated with higher self-concept and were reported to lessen BFLPE somewhat, though effects were generally modest. Overall, the BFLPE generalized widely across countries and individual differences.
